narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30 and an LSAT score of 36, you've clearly put in the effort to build a strong academic foundation, including ranking in the second decile for your Year 3 grades and first decile in Year 1 at your school, while coming from a middle-income family and planning to attend full-time. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 44% chance of failure for you—high enough that it tipped the decision toward rejecting your application to protect the program's bar passage rates. This prediction wasn't random; it was driven primarily by how your profile stacks up against patterns from thousands of past students. The most influential factor pulling against you was your Year 3 decile ranking, which is lower than average and pushed the model toward seeing a higher risk of bar failure. Right behind that, your Year 1 decile also contributed noticeably in the same direction, suggesting early performance signals mattered a lot here. On a brighter note, your LSAT score, which is right around the typical level, strongly worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward success, as did your undergraduate GPA that exceeds the average. Your full-time enrollment plan and middle-income background provided smaller but positive boosts too. Compared to typical students who pass, your LSAT and GPA align well or better, but your school deciles are on the lower side of what's common for strong bar performers, which weighed heavily in this close call. While this rejection stings, especially with your promising test scores, it's a signal to reflect on those undergraduate deciles. I encourage you to consider retaking the LSAT to push it even higher, seeking targeted advising to boost future decile rankings, or exploring programs with more flexible admissions criteria. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact areas— you've got the foundation, and with some strategic next steps, you can turn this around. Keep pushing forward; your story isn't over.
